1.synthesis: 'But God remembered Noah and all the beasts and all the livestock that were with him in the ark. And God made a wind blow over the earth, and the waters subsided. The fountains of the deep and the windows of the heavens were closed, the rain from the heavens was restrained, and the waters receded from the earth continually' (vv.1–3). The pivot of the flood narrative is the simplest and most theologically concentrated sentence: 'God remembered Noah.' The Hebrew verb zākar (to remember) does not mean God forgot — it is the covenant-action verb by which YHWH intervenes on behalf of his covenant partner. The waters subside because God acts: a wind (rûaḥ) passes over the waters, directly echoing the Spirit's hovering over the waters in Genesis 1:2. The un-creation is reversing. Noah's release of the raven and the dove — the dove returning first with nothing, then with an olive leaf, then not returning at all — provides the narrative's bird-sign of restoration.
1.historical_context: Divine 'remembering' (wayyizkōr) in the OT consistently triggers covenantal intervention: 'God remembered Rachel and opened her womb' (Genesis 30:22); 'God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham' (Exodus 2:24). The same verb drives key turns in the Psalms (Psalm 105:8: 'He remembers his covenant forever') and prophetic assurance (Jeremiah 31:34). The wind God sends (rûaḥ) over the waters (v.1) precisely echoes Genesis 1:2's Spirit hovering over the waters — the same creative agent that brought order from chaos in creation now reverses the un-creation of the flood. The raven (v.7) — an unclean scavenging bird — does not return; the dove (vv.8–12) — associated with purity and peace in later OT usage — becomes the three-movement sign of the earth's recovery. The Gilgamesh epic similarly uses three birds (dove, swallow, raven) but in different order, suggesting a common tradition reworked.
1.literary_structure: The section is structured as a reversal-parallel to ch7's flood-onset. Where ch7 recorded the opening of the fountains and windows (7:11), ch8 records their closing (v.2). Where ch7 dated the onset (7:11), ch8 dates the subsiding (vv.3–5). The bird-sequence (vv.6–12) is the narrative's tension-and-release mechanism: three dispatches of the dove at seven-day intervals — no landing, olive leaf, no return — provide the observable sign of land. The divine 'remembering' of v.1 is the section's theological anchor: everything that follows is the out-working of that single act of covenant-attention.
1.theological_application: Gill on 'God remembered Noah' (v.1): 'not that God had forgotten Noah, for he does not, and cannot forget his creatures, properly speaking; but this is said after the manner of men, and as it might have seemed to Noah, being shut up in the ark for so long a time … but God had him in mind, and in his heart, and thought upon him for good, and was ready to show him the mercy and kindness promised.' Matthew Henry: 'God remembered Noah. This is an expression after the manner of men: his compassionate concern for his distressed people; he never really forgets them; but the return of his favour after a time of trouble is like his remembering them. Note, none of God's people are forgotten of God, though they may sometimes think themselves neglected.' The wind (v.1) and the closing of the fountains (v.2) are acts of divine sovereignty: the same God who opened the waters closes them. The preservation of Noah is entirely from first to last an act of divine remembering.
1.christology: The divine 'remembering' that turns the flood is the OT pattern for what becomes explicit in the NT: God does not abandon those who are in covenant with him. Peter meditates on the Noah-pattern: 'the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trials' (2 Peter 2:9), citing Noah as the paradigm. The wind/Spirit that reversed the un-creation (v.1) prefigures the Spirit's role in the new creation: 'he who raised Christ Jesus from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through his Spirit who dwells in you' (Romans 8:11). The dove that returns with the olive leaf is the dove-form of the Spirit at Jesus's baptism (Matthew 3:16) — the bird that signals the end of judgment and the beginning of the new age recurs at the transition moment of the new covenant's inauguration.

13.synthesis: 'In the six hundred and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried from off the earth. And Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked, and behold, the face of the ground was dry' (v.13). The New Year's Day drying of the earth has the character of a new beginning — a new year, a new world. But Noah waits: despite seeing the dry ground, he remains in the ark until 'God said to Noah, "Go out from the ark, you and your wife, and your sons and your sons' wives with you. Bring out with you every living thing"' (vv.15–17). The exit is as ordered as the entrance: commanded by God, comprehensive in scope, family by family. The world Noah steps into is the same world materially but transformed morally — the violence of ch6 has been judged.
13.historical_context: The precise dating of the earth's drying (first month, first day of the 601st year — one year and eleven days after the flood's onset) gives the flood narrative an unusual documentary character. The calendar marks in the flood narrative (6:1: year 600, month 2, day 17; 8:4: month 7, day 17; 8:5: month 10, day 1; 8:13: year 601, month 1, day 1; 8:14: month 2, day 27) suggest a logbook structure, consistent with the narrative's claim to historical specificity. The command to 'go out' (v.16) and 'bring out with you every living thing' (v.17) mirrors the ark-loading command of 6:19–20 in reverse, and includes the explicit reproduction mandate: 'be fruitful and multiply on the earth' — the creation-blessing of Genesis 1:22 and Genesis 1:28 is restated for the post-flood world.
13.literary_structure: The two-verse transition (vv.13–14: earth dry by day 1 of month 1; completely dry by day 27 of month 2) creates a pause before the exit command. Noah does not leave the ark on his own initiative — he waits for God's word. This patient obedience mirrors his entering: he entered at God's command, and he exits at God's command. The exit-commission of vv.15–17 restates the creation-mandate: 'be fruitful and multiply on the earth' (v.17) is the new-creation's first command, confirming that the post-flood world is a genuine new beginning under the covenant. The family-unit structure of the exit (v.18: Noah, sons, wife, sons' wives) mirrors the entry structure of 7:13.
13.theological_application: Gill on God's command to exit (v.16): 'God said unto Noah, go forth of the ark — though the earth was dry, yet Noah did not go forth until God gave him leave and orders so to do; he waited patiently for the divine order, not daring to act without it, which showed great faith and obedience to the word of God.' Matthew Henry: 'Noah stayed until God bade him go out … Note, we must take the time of our removes and changes from God; we must follow providence, and neither go before it nor run away from it, but wait till God opens a door, and then go through it.' The patience of Noah's waiting — seeing the dry ground but not departing without divine command — is the posture of those who live by faith: the visible sign of readiness is not sufficient; the word of God is the authority for action.
13.christology: The reproduction mandate of v.17 ('be fruitful and multiply') reinstated at the flood's end recapitulates the creation mandate — and points forward to the ultimate fruitfulness promised through the seed of Abraham. The new world Noah steps into is ordered and clean, but sin is not eliminated — it surfaces almost immediately in ch9. The flood-judgment does not solve the sin-problem; it foreshadows the solution. What the flood could not do — create a truly new heart — the new covenant promises through the Spirit: 'I will give you a new heart' (Ezekiel 36:26). The exit from the ark into the renewed world is the type of the resurrection into the new creation — the emergence from death-through-judgment into the new life of the covenant.

20.synthesis: 'Then Noah built an altar to the LORD and took some of every clean animal and some of every clean bird and offered burnt offerings on the altar. And when the LORD smelled the pleasing aroma, the LORD said in his heart, "I will never again curse the ground because of man, for the intention of man's heart is evil from his youth. Neither will I ever again strike down every living creature as I have done. While the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night, shall not cease"' (vv.20–22). The first post-flood act is worship: before building a house, before planting a vineyard (that comes in ch9), Noah builds an altar. The divine response is a unilateral resolution — not a covenant yet (that comes in ch9), but a commitment within God's own deliberation: never again will he curse the ground for man's sake; never again will he destroy every living creature. Remarkably, the reason given is the same as the diagnosis that prompted the flood: 'the intention of man's heart is evil from his youth' — yet now the same fact produces mercy rather than judgment.
20.historical_context: The burnt offering (ʿōlāh) — whole animal consumed on the altar — is the earliest form of sacrifice recorded in Genesis. The 'pleasing aroma' (rêaḥ nîḥōaḥ) is the standard Levitical expression for an acceptable sacrifice, appearing over 40 times in the Mosaic legislation (Leviticus 1:9, 13, 17, etc.). The Gilgamesh parallel has Utnapishtim offering sacrifice after the flood, with the gods gathering around it 'like flies' — the Genesis account uses the same literary structure (sacrifice after flood, divine response) but transforms the theological content: one sovereign God, not squabbling gods. The divine resolve of vv.21–22 ('never again … while the earth remains') grounds the regularity of the natural order in a covenant commitment, not in natural necessity. The promise of seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, day and night is the foundation of the natural order on which all human civilization depends.
20.literary_structure: The three-verse section is brief but structurally pivotal: it is the first human act after the flood (v.20), the first occurrence of 'altar' (mizbēaḥ) in Genesis (all prior sacrifices were implied, as Abel's offering in 4:4), and the first explicit divine inner deliberation in the post-flood narrative (v.21: 'the LORD said in his heart'). The paradox of v.21 is the section's theological crux: the same diagnosis (evil heart) that justified the flood now justifies mercy — not because the sin is less serious but because the flood has revealed that judgment cannot change the heart. The natural-order promise of v.22 grounds all subsequent agricultural civilization in covenant faithfulness.
20.theological_application: Gill on the pleasing aroma: 'not the smell of burning flesh, but the spiritual savour of the type and antitype; Noah's sacrifice was a type of Christ's, and the acceptance of that, and the good will of God towards man for the sake of it.' On the 'evil from his youth' rationale for mercy: 'the same reason that is given for the destruction of the old world … is now given as a reason why God will no more destroy the world; not that God approves of this wickedness, or winks at it, but knowing that this evil nature of man cannot be altered by such judgments, he will not repeat them.' Matthew Henry: 'The resolution God here makes proceeds purely from his own goodness, and is not grounded on any merit in Noah or his sacrifice, but on God's own good will … the pleas of mercy prevail above those of justice, and God resolves no more to destroy the earth by a flood.' The mercy of v.21 is pure sovereign grace: knowing that man's heart is evil, God resolves not to judge by flood but to bear with it.
20.christology: The 'pleasing aroma' of Noah's sacrifice points forward to the one offering that perfectly satisfied — Christ's self-offering: 'Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2). Gill makes the connection explicit: 'Noah's sacrifice was a type of Christ's.' The reason God can sustain the natural order despite human evil (v.22) is the same reason he can sustain the covenant with sinners — not because of human improvement but because of a sufficient sacrifice. The promise of v.22 (seedtime and harvest shall not cease) grounds the reliability of creation in the covenant — and Paul grounds the reliability of the covenant in the faithfulness of Christ: 'all the promises of God find their Yes in him' (2 Corinthians 1:20).
